ALL-AI-PIPELINE-INTEGRITY.gap: No framework treats an MLflow model artifact / Recipe as untrusted executable code requiring provenance verification and sandboxed loading.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: MLflow model registries are shared across teams on trusted-collaborator assumptions; model artifacts are loaded without provenance verification or sandboxing, and no patch exists because the format is inherently executable.

new_control_requirements.name: UNTRUSTED-MODEL-ARTIFACT-LOADING
new_control_requirements.description: Treat ML model artifacts as untrusted code: never load .keras / pickle-based models from untrusted sources, verify provenance, prefer safe formats (e.g. safetensors), and load untrusted models only in a sandboxed, network-isolated, least-privilege environment. Upgrade Keras to the fixed release (>= 3.8.0 for CVE-2025-1550; past 3.10.0 for the CVE-2025-8747 safe_mode bypass) and do not rely on safe_mode alone — it was bypassable. The distinguishing test: load an attacker-crafted .keras archive with safe_mode enabled on a sandboxed instance and confirm no code executes.
new_control_requirements.evidence: https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-1550

CVE-2025-27520.name: BentoML serde.py Insecure Deserialization Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution

attack_vector.description: BentoML 1.3.4 through 1.4.2 reconstructs an attacker-supplied serialized object in serde.py from a request without validation, giving any unauthenticated user remote code execution - the same insecure-deserialization class that 1.2.5 fixed, recurring on the serde.py path.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated)
attack_vector.complexity: low
attack_vector.ai_factor: The abused surface is BentoML, a model-serving / inference framework. The lesson: a model server must never reconstruct an untrusted serialized object from a request - the same inference-path deserialization class as the ShadowMQ / vLLM entries, here on the HTTP serving API, and it recurred in BentoML after the first fix.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation closed one deserialization path (1.2.5) but the class recurred (serde.py, fixed 1.4.3) - the fix did not generalize to all request-deserialization sinks.

NIST-800-53-SI-10.gap: No input validation distinguishes a safe payload from a deserialization-gadget object at the model-serving endpoint.

ALL-AI-PIPELINE-INTEGRITY.gap: No framework treats a model-serving framework's request-deserialization path as a privileged execution surface.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Model-serving frameworks are deployed for inference throughput on trusted-network assumptions; their request-deserialization paths are not treated as untrusted-input boundaries.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-INFERENCE-IPC-DESERIALIZATION-SAFETY
new_control_requirements.description: AI inference engines must use a safe serializer (e.g. JSON) for IPC/socket communication, never deserialize untrusted serialized objects, authenticate socket peers, and isolate the channel on a trusted network segment. Because the insecure primitive spread by code reuse, the control must be applied across every inference engine in the estate, not one at a time. Apply the project fix (NVIDIA TensorRT-LLM), and for vLLM keep the legacy V0 engine disabled. The distinguishing test: send a crafted serialized object to the inference engine's socket from an unauthorized peer on a staging instance and confirm it is rejected, not deserialized.
new_control_requirements.evidence: https://nvidia.custhelp.com/app/answers/detail/a_id/5648

CVE-2023-6038.name: H2O-3 REST API Unauthenticated Local File Inclusion (Arbitrary File Read)

attack_vector.description: H2O-3's REST API exposes a file-import path with no authorization check, so an unauthenticated remote attacker reads arbitrary files on the host with the H2O-3 process's permissions (Local File Inclusion).
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated)
attack_vector.complexity: low
attack_vector.ai_factor: The abused surface is H2O-3, an open-source ML/AutoML platform. The lesson: an ML platform's control plane is a privileged surface - its REST API must authenticate every endpoint; a 'trusted environment' deployment assumption is not a control.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: The H2O-3 REST API does not authenticate callers before serving a file-import path; 'trusted environment' is assumed, not enforced.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection does not treat the unauthenticated ML-platform REST API as an exposed surface.

ALL-AI-PIPELINE-INTEGRITY.gap: No framework requires authenticating every endpoint of an ML platform's control plane / REST API.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: H2O-3 is deployed for data-science productivity on trusted-network assumptions; its dashboard / REST API is frequently exposed without authentication, and the vendor ships no fix (trusted-environment-by-design).

new_control_requirements.name: AI-COMPUTE-CONTROL-PLANE-AUTHENTICATION
new_control_requirements.description: An AI compute framework's job/control API must authenticate every caller; 'deploy only on a trusted network' is an assumption, not a control, and must not substitute for authentication. Enable Ray token authentication (2.52.0+), never expose the dashboard / Job Submission API to untrusted networks, front it with an authenticating proxy, and treat any internet-exposed cluster as compromised (rotate model artifacts and cloud credentials). The distinguishing test: from the public internet, attempt to reach the Ray dashboard (default 8265) and submit a job unauthenticated on a staging cluster; it must be refused.
new_control_requirements.evidence: https://atlas.mitre.org/studies/AML.CS0023

CVE-2024-24591.name: ClearML Client SDK Dataset Path Traversal Arbitrary File Write

attack_vector.description: ClearML's client SDK writes dataset entries without path containment on retrieval, so a maliciously uploaded dataset with absolute / ../ entries writes files to arbitrary locations on the retrieving user's system (escalating to code execution by overwriting startup/config files).
attack_vector.privileges_required: low-to-none (upload access to a shared project; victim must retrieve the artifact - UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: low
attack_vector.ai_factor: The abused surface is the MLOps / experiment-tracking layer that moves artifacts and datasets between data scientists. The lesson: an MLOps platform is a supply-chain channel - uploaded artifacts are untrusted code/file payloads and must never be auto-deserialized or extracted without containment.

NIST-800-53-SI-10.gap: No path validation is applied to dataset entries before the MLOps SDK extracts them.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality is not enforced: dataset extraction writes entries without containing them to the cache directory.

ALL-AI-PIPELINE-INTEGRITY.gap: No framework treats an MLOps platform's uploaded datasets as an untrusted file-write surface.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: MLOps platforms are deployed for collaboration on trusted-team assumptions; their artifact/dataset retrieval paths are not treated as untrusted-content boundaries.

new_control_requirements.name: MLOPS-EXPERIMENT-ARTIFACT-TRUST-BOUNDARY
new_control_requirements.description: An MLOps / experiment-tracking platform (ClearML, and the class: Weights & Biases, MLflow artifact stores, model registries) must treat every uploaded artifact and dataset as untrusted third-party content, because these platforms move artifacts between collaborators automatically. On retrieval the client SDK must NOT reconstruct artifacts through an unsafe object-deserialization routine (use a safe loader / explicit schema, or sandbox the deserialization), and must constrain dataset extraction to a contained directory (reject absolute and ../ traversal entries). Run the SDK least-privilege and only pull from trusted projects. The distinguishing test: upload an artifact carrying a deserialization-gadget payload and a dataset with a ../ entry to a staging project, retrieve them from a separate client, and confirm neither runs code nor writes outside the cache directory - a 'secured MLOps' posture that still auto-deserializes or extracts uploaded content without containment is exposed.
new_control_requirements.evidence: https://hiddenlayer.com/innovation-hub/not-so-clear-how-mlops-solutions-can-muddy-the-waters-of-your-supply-chain/

CVE-2026-33017.name: Langflow Public Flow-Build Endpoint Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution

attack_vector.description: After the CVE-2025-3248 fix, Langflow's public flow execution surface remained exploitable: the unauthenticated /api/v1/build_public_tmp/{flow_id}/flow endpoint runs flow-supplied Python through an unsandboxed dynamic-execution sink, giving remote code execution again; fixed in 1.9.0.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated public endpoint)
attack_vector.complexity: low
attack_vector.ai_factor: The abused surface is a widely deployed visual LLM app/agent builder. The lesson: an LLM app builder's flow endpoints are code-execution surfaces, so every one must authenticate and sandbox - and the fix must cover the whole class of endpoints, not the single route that was reported (the Langflow pattern recurred on a new endpoint after the first KEV-listed fix).

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: A second public flow endpoint reaches a code-execution sink without authentication - the access-control gap recurred on a new route after the first fix.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality is not enforced: a public endpoint runs flow-supplied code through a dynamic-execution sink without sandboxing.

ALL-AI-PIPELINE-INTEGRITY.gap: No framework requires enumerating EVERY flow validate/build/run endpoint of an LLM app builder as an execution surface - so the same class recurred on a new endpoint.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: LLM app builders are stood up for rapid prototyping on trusted-network assumptions and exposed without authentication; their flow endpoints are not treated as code-execution surfaces.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-APP-BUILDER-EXECUTION-ENDPOINT-AUTH-AND-SANDBOX
new_control_requirements.description: A visual LLM app/agent builder (Langflow, Flowise, and similar) must authenticate every endpoint that can reach a code-execution path - validate-code, flow-build, flow-run, public-flow endpoints - and must never run flow-supplied or request-supplied code through a compile-and-run / dynamic-evaluation path with host privileges. Place the builder behind authenticated access control, never expose it to untrusted networks, and sandbox any code the platform executes on a user's behalf (no filesystem/network/process access beyond the flow's intent). The distinguishing test: send an unauthenticated request to each flow validate/build/run endpoint on a staging instance with a payload that attempts a non-flow action (a shell or network call) and confirm it is refused before any code runs - paper 'AI platform' policies that leave a public endpoint wired to a code-execution sink still permit unauthenticated RCE.
new_control_requirements.evidence: https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-vwmf-pq79-vjvx

CVE-2024-12366.name: PandasAI Prompt Injection to Remote Code Execution

attack_vector.description: PandasAI's chat interface turns a natural-language question into Python and runs it against DataFrames; it does not separate analytical input from injected instructions, so prompt injection generates and executes arbitrary Python, escaping the intended sandbox (RCE).
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, no user interaction)
attack_vector.complexity: low
attack_vector.ai_factor: The flaw is intrinsic to the AI pipeline: the agent's purpose is to turn natural language into executed code, so prompt injection is the exploit primitive. The lesson - LLM-generated code is attacker-controllable code and must be sandboxed, never run with host privileges.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection does not treat an LLM agent's generate-and-run-code path as a code-execution channel.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality is not enforced - the code-execution / visualization path is on by default rather than sandboxed or disabled for untrusted input.

ALL-PROMPT-INJECTION-ACCESS-CONTROL.gap: No framework requires that prompt-injectable natural-language input be denied a path to code execution in NL-to-code/SQL agents.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: NL-to-code/SQL agents are adopted for analyst productivity and run model-generated code by design; their codegen path is rarely sandboxed and the natural-language input is not treated as untrusted.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-NL-TO-CODE-AGENT-EXECUTION-ISOLATION
new_control_requirements.description: An LLM data-analysis agent that generates and executes code or SQL from natural language (text-to-SQL, text-to-Python, charting agents) must treat BOTH the natural-language question and any analyzed data as untrusted, prompt-injectable input, and must never run model-generated code with host or network privileges. Disable code-execution/visualization paths by default for untrusted input, run generated code only in a hardened sandbox (no filesystem/network/process access beyond the dataset), enforce least functionality, and validate or constrain the generated artifact before execution. The distinguishing test: send an analytical question containing an injected instruction to emit non-analytical code (e.g. a shell/file/network call) on a staging agent and confirm the agent refuses to execute it - paper 'AI security' policies that do not sandbox the generate-and-run path still permit RCE.
new_control_requirements.evidence: https://www.kb.cert.org/vuls/id/148244

CVE-2025-67818.name: Weaviate Backup Restore ZipSlip Path Traversal

attack_vector.description: Weaviate OSS does not constrain backup entry paths on restore, so a write-capable attacker uses absolute / ../ paths to escape the restore root (CWE-22 ZipSlip) and create or overwrite arbitrary host files.
attack_vector.privileges_required: data-write access (NVD PR:H)
attack_vector.complexity: low
attack_vector.ai_factor: The abused surface is a widely used vector database - the RAG persistence layer that stores embeddings and source data behind LLM applications. The lesson: vector databases are sensitive, RCE-bearing data stores; their file/archive-handling paths must be containment-checked.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Does not track the vector database (RAG persistence layer) as managed, RCE-bearing software.

NIST-800-53-SI-10.gap: Path validation is not applied to backup entry paths on restore.

ALL-AI-PIPELINE-INTEGRITY.gap: No framework treats the vector database as a sensitive RAG store whose backup/file paths must be containment-checked.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Vector databases are deployed as convenience RAG infrastructure on trusted-network assumptions; their request and backup paths are not hardened.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-RUNTIME-API-PATH-TRAVERSAL-VALIDATION
new_control_requirements.description: An AI application's file/path-bearing inputs - including archive (backup) entry paths on extraction - must be canonicalized and constrained to the intended directory before any write (reject absolute paths and ../ traversal / ZipSlip). Upgrade Weaviate OSS to the fixed release on your maintained branch (1.30.20 / 1.31.19 / 1.32.16 / 1.33.4), restrict who can insert data or trigger restores, and run least-privilege. This is the same path-traversal class as the Ollama / AnythingLLM entries. The distinguishing test: restore a backup containing a ../ entry on a staging instance and confirm it is rejected, not written outside the restore root.
new_control_requirements.evidence: https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-7v39-2hx7-7c43

CVE-2026-26190.name: Milvus Port 9091 Missing Authentication / Weak Default Token

attack_vector.description: Milvus (CWE-306 missing authentication on port 9091 with weak default tokens) lets an unauthenticated network attacker reach the vector database's operations and data, bypassing authentication.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (NVD/CNA AV:N / PR:N) - unauthenticated
attack_vector.complexity: low (AC:L)
attack_vector.ai_factor: The abused surface is the vector database - the RAG persistence layer that stores embeddings and the source documents (often PII) behind LLM applications. The lesson: vector databases are sensitive data stores, not caches; every API/management port (including metrics ports like 9091) must authenticate, default tokens must be replaced, and the DB must not be network-exposed. An auth bypass here exposes RAG data and enables retrieval poisoning.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: Authentication is not enforced on the vector database's API/management surface.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Does not track the vector database (RAG persistence layer) as managed, auth-bypass-bearing software.

ALL-AI-PIPELINE-INTEGRITY.gap: No framework treats the vector database as a sensitive RAG data store whose API/management ports must authenticate.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Vector databases are deployed as convenience RAG infrastructure on trusted-network assumptions, often with default tokens and exposed management ports.

new_control_requirements.name: VECTOR-DB-AUTHENTICATION-ENFORCEMENT
new_control_requirements.description: A vector database storing RAG embeddings and source data must enforce authentication on every API and management/metrics port (including ports like Milvus 9091), reject forged/missing auth, replace default tokens, and never be exposed to untrusted networks. Upgrade Milvus to a patched release (2.5.27 / 2.6.10). The distinguishing test: from an unauthenticated client, attempt forged-header access to the Proxy and direct access to the metrics/management port on a staging instance and confirm both are refused.
new_control_requirements.evidence: https://github.com/milvus-io/milvus/security/advisories/GHSA-7ppg-37fh-vcr6

CVE-2024-4889.name: BerriAI LiteLLM Config Code Injection via UI_LOGO_PATH / KMS

attack_vector.description: BerriAI LiteLLM (CWE-94 code injection via the secret-management config path) on the LLM proxy/gateway lets admin-influenced config reach a dynamic-evaluation path and execute code on the credential-bearing proxy.
attack_vector.privileges_required: admin-level config influence (NVD PR:H)
attack_vector.complexity: low (NVD AC:L)
attack_vector.ai_factor: The abused surface is the LLM proxy/gateway - the shared chokepoint that holds provider API keys for many models and teams. The lesson: an LLM gateway is a high-value credential store, so its request and config planes must be isolated from the secrets (allow-list egress, no dynamic evaluation of config), or a single SSRF / config flaw becomes mass key theft or proxy RCE. CVE-2024-6587 was the SSRF link of a Pwn2Own full-chain RCE against LiteLLM.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: Authenticator (API key) management does not isolate the gateway's stored provider keys from request-controlled egress.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Does not track the LLM proxy/gateway as managed, credential-bearing software.

ALL-AI-PIPELINE-INTEGRITY.gap: No framework treats the LLM gateway as a high-value credential store whose request/config plane must be isolated from the secrets.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: LLM gateways concentrate provider keys but are deployed as convenience infrastructure; their request/config planes are not isolated from the credential store.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-GATEWAY-CREDENTIAL-STORE-ISOLATION
new_control_requirements.description: The LLM proxy/gateway must isolate its stored provider credentials from request- and config-controlled flows: allow-list permitted provider endpoints (reject attacker-supplied api_base), never forward stored keys to unvalidated destinations, disable dynamic evaluation of configuration, and keep the admin/config surface off untrusted networks. Upgrade BerriAI LiteLLM to a patched release (1.44.16+). The distinguishing test: send a /chat/completions request with an attacker api_base, and an admin config with a remote UI_LOGO_PATH, to a staging gateway and confirm no key egress and no code execution.
new_control_requirements.evidence: https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-423v-966v-frxg

CVE-2023-44467.name: LangChain-Experimental PALChain dunder-import Code Execution (CVE-2023-36258 bypass)

attack_vector.description: A LangChain experimental chain (CWE-94 prompt-generated Python with an incomplete builtin denylist) turns prompt-influenced input into executed Python, so prompt injection becomes arbitrary code execution in the LLM application.
attack_vector.privileges_required: control of the input prompt to the LLM application (NVD AV:N)
attack_vector.complexity: low-to-moderate (steer the chain's generated/evaluated code via the prompt)
attack_vector.ai_factor: This is an AI-native attack surface: the LLM-application prompt is the injection vector, and chains that execute generated code (PALChain, VectorSQLDatabaseChain, LLMMathChain) convert prompt injection into RCE. The CVE-2023-36258 -> CVE-2023-44467 sequence shows denylisting builtins is an incomplete fix; code generated under attacker influence must be sandboxed or not executed at all.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Does not track LLM-orchestration libraries' code-executing chains as RCE-bearing software, nor that builtin denylists are an incomplete fix.

NIST-800-53-SI-10.gap: Input validation does not cover prompt-influenced strings an LLM chain turns into executable code.

ALL-PROMPT-INJECTION-ACCESS-CONTROL.gap: No framework treats prompt-influenced input to a code-executing LLM chain as untrusted code; prompt injection becomes arbitrary code execution.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: LLM apps wire in code-executing chains for convenience and trust the prompt; denylist-based mitigations are assumed sufficient despite documented bypasses.

new_control_requirements.name: LLM-CHAIN-GENERATED-CODE-EXECUTION-CONTROL
new_control_requirements.description: LLM chains/agents that execute generated or prompt-influenced code (PALChain, VectorSQLDatabaseChain, LLMMathChain, Python/Pandas agents) must sandbox execution in an isolated, least-privilege, network-restricted environment or disable code execution entirely; builtin/function denylists are insufficient (the CVE-2023-36258 fix was bypassed via the dunder-import builtin in CVE-2023-44467). Upgrade langchain-experimental to 0.0.306 or later, and never expose a code-executing chain to untrusted prompts. The distinguishing test: send a prompt-injection payload that requests code execution to a staging chain and confirm it is sandboxed/refused, not run in-process.
new_control_requirements.evidence: https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-gjjr-63x4-v8cq

CVE-2025-22224.name: VMware ESXi/Workstation VMCI TOCTOU → VMX Host Code Execution

attack_vector.description: Local administrative privilege on a guest VM exploits a TOCTOU race in VMCI (Virtual Machine Communication Interface), leading to OOB write in the VMX host process — VM escape to hypervisor. Huntress PDB-path evidence ('2024_02_19' folder) suggests the exploit chain pre-dated disclosure by ~12 months. Used in ransomware operations per CISA February 2026 follow-up.
attack_vector.privileges_required: local guest-admin in a VM on the host
attack_vector.complexity: moderate (TOCTOU race; in-wild PoC predates disclosure)
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered. Microsoft Threat Intelligence Center disclosure; no AI tool credit.

NIST-800-53-SC-39.gap: Hypervisor isolation control assumes guest-to-host boundary is intact; TOCTOU race breaks it.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.21.gap: Network segregation assumed at the virtualization layer; VM-escape sidesteps.

NIS2-Art21-business-continuity.gap: Hypervisor compromise blast radius covers all tenants on the host.

FedRAMP-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection assumes hypervisor as trust anchor.

new_control_requirements.name: CISA-KEV-RESPONSE-SLA
new_control_requirements.description: 4h KEV-listing mitigation SLA on the hypervisor estate.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-22224 — KEV-listed 2025-03-04; ransomware-confirmed in-wild use.

new_control_requirements.name: HYPERVISOR-VM-ESCAPE-TENANCY-ASSUMPTION
new_control_requirements.description: Multi-tenant virtualization must treat any guest-admin as a hypervisor adversary; KEV-listed hypervisor CVEs trigger 24h SLA regardless of tenancy.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-22224 — ~12 months of pre-disclosure in-wild use blew through every tenancy assumption.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Hypervisor patch cadence is structurally slow due to maintenance-window scheduling; ransomware operators target the window directly.

CVE-2025-22225.name: VMware ESXi Arbitrary Kernel Write (VM-escape chain, ransomware-active)

attack_vector.description: Arbitrary kernel-write primitive inside the VMX process used to write to ESXi kernel memory and execute as the hypervisor. Chained with CVE-2025-22224 (TOCTOU) and CVE-2025-22226 (memory leak) for full VM-escape → hypervisor-management access.
attack_vector.privileges_required: code execution inside the VMX process (typically from CVE-2025-22224 chain step)
attack_vector.complexity: moderate (chain component; ransomware-confirmed)
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered. Microsoft Threat Intelligence Center co-disclosure; no AI tool credit.

NIST-800-53-SC-39.gap: Same hypervisor-isolation gap as CVE-2025-22224; ransomware-confirmed.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.21.gap: Network segregation assumed at the virtualization layer; chain breaks it.

DORA-Art10.gap: ICT third-party concentration risk realised at the hypervisor layer.

new_control_requirements.name: CISA-KEV-RESPONSE-SLA
new_control_requirements.description: 4h KEV-listing mitigation SLA; same VMSA-2025-0004 cluster as CVE-2025-22224.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-22225 — KEV-listed 2025-03-04; CISA Feb 2026 follow-up confirmed ransomware exploitation.

new_control_requirements.name: HYPERVISOR-VM-ESCAPE-TENANCY-ASSUMPTION
new_control_requirements.description: Same hypervisor tenancy posture as CVE-2025-22224.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-22225 — chain component in the same ransomware-confirmed exploitation cluster.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Same hypervisor patch-cadence gap as CVE-2025-22224.

CVE-2025-22226.name: VMware ESXi HGFS Memory Leak (VM-escape chain helper)

attack_vector.description: HGFS (Host Guest File System) memory leak provides the heap-address oracle used to weaponise CVE-2025-22224 + CVE-2025-22225. Local guest-admin reads VMX-process memory via the leak primitive and supplies heap addresses for the TOCTOU + arbitrary-write CVEs in the same chain. Broadcom characterised as 'observed in attacks'.
attack_vector.privileges_required: local guest-admin
attack_vector.complexity: low (information-disclosure read primitive)
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered. Microsoft Threat Intelligence Center co-disclosure; no AI tool credit.

NIST-800-53-SC-28.gap: Memory-protection control assumes process isolation; leak escapes.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.24.gap: In-transit / at-rest crypto irrelevant to in-memory disclosure.

FedRAMP-SC-4.gap: Information in shared resources — direct violation.

new_control_requirements.name: CISA-KEV-RESPONSE-SLA
new_control_requirements.description: 4h KEV-listing mitigation SLA; same VMSA-2025-0004 cluster.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-22226 — KEV-listed 2025-03-04; the heap-address oracle that makes the chain reliable.

new_control_requirements.name: HYPERVISOR-VM-ESCAPE-TENANCY-ASSUMPTION
new_control_requirements.description: Same hypervisor tenancy posture as CVE-2025-22224.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-22226 — without this leak the rest of the chain is unreliable; chain-component classification still triggers the tenancy-assumption control.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Memory-leak-class CVEs on hypervisors are commonly downgraded by operator patch programs; chain-context elevates them.

MAL-2024-PYPI-ULTRALYTICS-XMRIG.name: ultralytics PyPI Compromise → XMRig Cryptominer (60M-download AI library)

attack_vector.description: GitHub Actions script-injection in the ultralytics build pipeline allowed openimbot to inject a post-install downloader after code review. Resulting wheel pulled XMRig from attacker infrastructure. ultralytics 8.3.41 and 8.3.42 (the first 'remediation' release inadvertently re-shipped the malicious code) — clean release 8.3.43. ~60M monthly downloads; the package powers many production CV / model-training pipelines.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (any developer / CI runs `pip install ultralytics`)
attack_vector.complexity: low for downstream consumers; build-pipeline injection required upstream
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered. ReversingLabs + Wiz + HiddenLayer concurrent ecosystem-telemetry detection; no AI tool credit.

NIST-800-218-SSDF-PO.4.2.gap: Build-environment hardening guidance generic; GitHub Actions script-injection class persists.

SLSA-3.gap: Build provenance attestation gap — the injected code shipped with valid attestation paths.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.30.gap: Outsourced development control does not address ecosystem-package build-pipeline injection.

EU-AI-Act-Art10.gap: Data governance — compromised AI library is in scope but framework lacks supply-chain control prescription.

OpenSSF-Scorecard-PinnedDependenciesID.gap: Float-version installs propagate compromise instantly; pin-by-hash mitigates only at deploy time.

new_control_requirements.name: GHA-WORKFLOW-SCRIPT-INJECTION-SINK-BAN
new_control_requirements.description: GitHub Actions workflows must ban dangerous expression sinks (run: with `${{ github.event.* }}` interpolation, etc.); CI must lint workflows for the injection class.
new_control_requirements.evidence: MAL-2024-PYPI-ULTRALYTICS-XMRIG — the original injection vector was a GHA expression-injection sink.

new_control_requirements.name: NPM-CI-IGNORE-SCRIPTS-DEFAULT
new_control_requirements.description: Equivalent posture for pip: `pip install --no-binary` / cached wheel review; reject post-install code execution by default in CI.
new_control_requirements.evidence: MAL-2024-PYPI-ULTRALYTICS-XMRIG — the malicious payload activated via post-install downloader.

new_control_requirements.name: MAINTAINER-TOKEN-COMPROMISE-RESPONSE-PLAYBOOK
new_control_requirements.description: Maintainer-token compromise must have a 24h response playbook; 8.3.42 re-shipped the malicious code because the response was rushed.
new_control_requirements.evidence: MAL-2024-PYPI-ULTRALYTICS-XMRIG — the inadvertent re-ship of malicious code in 8.3.42 demonstrates the absent playbook.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: AI-library supply chain is essentially unmanaged at SLSA-3 depth across audit-passing organisations; ~60M-download package compromise demonstrates the blast radius.

MAL-2026-RUBYGEMS-BUFFERZONECORP-SLEEPER.name: BufferZoneCorp RubyGems + Go Module Sleeper-to-Payload Credential Theft Campaign

attack_vector.description: Sleeper-to-payload supply chain attack: packages published with clean README + minimal functionality, silently updated to malicious payload after trust accrual. Ruby gems harvested env vars + SSH keys + AWS credentials + .npmrc + .netrc + GitHub CLI config + RubyGems credentials to a hidden C2 endpoint. Go modules modified GITHUB_ENV, poisoned GOPROXY, weakened checksum protections, tampered with go.sum, and planted fake `go` wrappers in workflow execution paths.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (any CI / dev runs `bundle install` / `go get`)
attack_vector.complexity: low for downstream; sustained over time upstream
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered. Socket.dev human research disclosure; no AI tool credit.

NIST-800-218-SSDF-PO.4.2.gap: Sleeper pattern defeats one-time package audit.

SLSA-3.gap: Build provenance ineffective against post-trust-accrual malicious update.

ISO-27001-2022-A.5.21.gap: Supplier security control depends on initial assessment; does not address temporal trust drift.

OpenSSF-Scorecard-PinnedDependenciesID.gap: Pinned dependency mitigates only if hash-pinned, not version-pinned.

NIS2-Art21-supply-chain.gap: Supply-chain control assumes initial vetting; sleeper pattern not enumerated.

new_control_requirements.name: ECOSYSTEM-PACKAGE-TEMPORAL-TRUST-DRIFT-DETECTION
new_control_requirements.description: Pin by content hash; monitor for behaviour deltas (new network egress, new postinstall scripts, new permissions); treat publisher-account churn as leading indicator.
new_control_requirements.evidence: MAL-2026-RUBYGEMS-BUFFERZONECORP-SLEEPER — sleeper pattern defeated audit and version-pinning.

new_control_requirements.name: NPM-CI-IGNORE-SCRIPTS-DEFAULT
new_control_requirements.description: Equivalent posture for Bundler / Go: refuse postinstall / init-hook execution from new or recently-updated dependencies by default in CI.
new_control_requirements.evidence: MAL-2026-RUBYGEMS-BUFFERZONECORP-SLEEPER — Go module init hooks rewrote GOPROXY and go.sum.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Temporal trust drift detection is essentially never deployed in audit-passing organisations; one-time supplier vetting is the norm.

MAL-2025-PYPI-COLORAMA-SOLANA-STEALER.name: PyPI Colorama Typosquat Campaign → Solana Credential / Crypto Stealer

attack_vector.description: 11 PyPI typosquats of `colorama` (>150M downloads/month) and `colorizr` published 2025-05-04 to 2025-05-24, split across four payload variants targeting the Solana ecosystem. Install-time code exfiltrates browser credentials, crypto wallets, Facebook/Telegram/Roblox session material, and Solana wallet artifacts.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (developer typo / autocomplete on `pip install`)
attack_vector.complexity: low
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered. Imperva + Checkmarx + Check Point ecosystem-telemetry detection; no AI tool credit.

NIST-800-218-SSDF-PO.4.2.gap: Typosquat detection not enumerated in standard SSDF practices.

ISO-27001-2022-A.5.21.gap: Supplier control does not address ecosystem-name-confusion class.

OpenSSF-Scorecard-PinnedDependenciesID.gap: Pin-by-hash mitigates only at deploy time; install-time typosquat persists.

GDPR-Art32.gap: Confidentiality breach via developer-endpoint compromise underspecified.

new_control_requirements.name: TYPOSQUAT-INSTALL-TIME-NAME-CONFUSION-GUARD
new_control_requirements.description: CI + developer workstations must run install-time typosquat guards (Levenshtein-distance check against top-N ecosystem packages, install-time prompt on first-seen package names).
new_control_requirements.evidence: MAL-2025-PYPI-COLORAMA-SOLANA-STEALER — 11 typosquats exfiltrated browser credentials and Solana wallets at install time.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Install-time typosquat guards are essentially never deployed at workstation depth; supplier-list controls do not enumerate the class.

CVE-2025-0133.name: Palo Alto Networks GlobalProtect Reflected XSS (XBOW AI-discovered)

attack_vector.description: Reflected XSS triggered via a crafted link processed by the GlobalProtect captive-portal. Executes JavaScript in the authenticated user's browser context, enabling credential-phishing originating from the legitimate VPN portal hostname. Affected over 2,000 internet-facing hosts at disclosure.
attack_vector.privileges_required: user interaction (click on crafted link to the legitimate portal)
attack_vector.complexity: low
attack_vector.ai_factor: XBOW autonomous-pentest AI discovered the bug during a HackerOne VDP engagement scoped to GlobalProtect. Notable because XBOW reached #1 on the HackerOne US Q2 2025 leaderboard ahead of every human researcher in the same program ranking — first publicly-attributed AI-tool CVE against Palo Alto.

NIST-800-53-SI-10.gap: Input validation assumes static-analysis coverage; AI-discovery surfaced bugs missed by conventional tooling.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.28.gap: Secure coding controls do not enumerate AI-assisted discovery as a positive defence modality.

OWASP-Top-10-2021-A03.gap: Injection class — XSS in security-control-plane software.

EU-AI-Act-Art15.gap: Robustness control does not address AI-assisted-discovery contribution credit.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-DISCOVERY-RESPONSE-SLA
new_control_requirements.description: AI-discovered vulnerabilities enter the patch program on the AI-discovery-date timestamp.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-0133 — XBOW disclosure cadence outpaces conventional bug-bounty triage.

new_control_requirements.name: PERIMETER-DEVICE-ZERODAY-SLA-TIER
new_control_requirements.description: Perimeter devices (VPN concentrators, gateways) require a separate compressed-SLA tier; XSS on the captive-portal hostname enables credential-phishing from the legitimate brand.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-0133 — over 2,000 internet-facing GlobalProtect hosts exposed at disclosure.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-DISCOVERY-CREDIT-IN-COMPLIANCE-EVIDENCE
new_control_requirements.description: Compliance evidence packs must record AI-tool contribution.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-0133 — XBOW topped the HackerOne leaderboard; no compliance attribution path exists for this contribution.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Perimeter-device XSS is commonly out of scope of organisational threat models for VPN concentrators; AI-discovery outpaces patch programs.

CVE-2025-59529.name: Avahi Simple Protocol Server Connection-Limit DoS (ZeroPath AI-discovered)

attack_vector.description: avahi-daemon Simple Protocol Server continues accepting clients after the configured connection limit should have engaged. Local or network-reachable attacker opens repeated connections; daemon ignores connection-cap configuration and exhausts file descriptors / memory, denying service-discovery on the host.
attack_vector.privileges_required: local or network-reachable (where avahi-daemon is exposed)
attack_vector.complexity: low
attack_vector.ai_factor: ZeroPath AI-SAST during code analysis of the Avahi project. Notable as a business-logic-class detection — the category most resistant to conventional pattern-based SAST and most accelerated by LLM-driven analysis.

NIST-800-53-SC-5.gap: Default-allow connection acceptance behaviour escapes generic DoS-protection control.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.9.gap: Configuration baseline assumes configured-limit-enforced; business-logic bypass not enumerated.

ENISA-IoT-security-baseline.gap: IoT-device service-discovery hardening unspecified for business-logic class.

EU-AI-Act-Art15.gap: Framework does not credit AI-assisted-defender finding.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-DISCOVERY-RESPONSE-SLA
new_control_requirements.description: AI-discovered vulnerabilities enter the patch program on the AI-discovery-date timestamp.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-59529 — ZeroPath disclosure cadence outpaces conventional SAST triage.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-DISCOVERY-CREDIT-IN-COMPLIANCE-EVIDENCE
new_control_requirements.description: Compliance evidence packs must record AI-tool contribution; business-logic-class detection is the AI-defender comparative advantage.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-59529 — ZeroPath business-logic-class detection has no SAMM / SSDF attribution.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Avahi is ubiquitous on Linux desktops / IoT; business-logic-class DoS is commonly downgraded by patch programs.

CVE-2025-55319.name: Visual Studio Code Agentic-AI Command Injection (ZeroPath AI-discovered)

attack_vector.description: Adversarial content in an AI tool response or external MCP server message reaches a shell-execution primitive inside VS Code's agentic integration, executing attacker-controlled commands as the developer.
attack_vector.privileges_required: ability to deliver adversarial content into the agent's tool / MCP response surface
attack_vector.complexity: moderate (high-AC per CVSS but trivial once the primitive is known)
attack_vector.ai_factor: ZeroPath AI-SAST analysing the VS Code agentic-AI surface. Doubly relevant: AI defender finding a bug in an AI-assistant surface; AI agent is also the weaponisation primitive (it transforms a crafted tool response into a shell command). Qualifies under both limbs of AGENTS.md Hard Rule #7.

NIST-AI-RMF-MEASURE-2.7.gap: Prompt-injection-to-shell pathway underspecified in AI-RMF measurement guidance.

EU-AI-Act-Art15.gap: Agentic-AI host-execution boundary not enumerated as a robustness control.

ISO-IEC-42001-AIMS-A.6.2.5.gap: AI lifecycle controls do not address IDE-resident agentic primitives.

OWASP-LLM-Top-10-LLM01.gap: Prompt injection enumerated; no IDE-host-execution requirement.

OWASP-LLM-Top-10-LLM07.gap: Insecure Plugin Design directly applicable to MCP integration class.

new_control_requirements.name: AGENTIC-IDE-HOST-EXECUTION-SANDBOX
new_control_requirements.description: Agentic AI in IDEs must route shell-execution through per-invocation confirmation or a sandbox isolated from developer credentials.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-55319 — adversarial tool / MCP content reached a shell primitive directly.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-TOOL-ACTION-AUTHORIZATION
new_control_requirements.description: AI tools must have explicitly scoped permissions; implied authorisation from context is insufficient.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-55319 — agent acted on adversarial content without explicit authorisation.

new_control_requirements.name: MCP-SERVER-CONFIG-ALLOWLIST
new_control_requirements.description: MCP server inventory + allowlist limits the adversarial-content surface that can reach the IDE agent.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-55319 — external MCP server messages were a documented delivery path.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-DISCOVERY-CREDIT-IN-COMPLIANCE-EVIDENCE
new_control_requirements.description: Record AI-defender contribution.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-55319 — ZeroPath disclosure; framework attribution gap.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Developer-workstation agentic-AI host-execution sandboxes are essentially non-existent across audit-passing organisations; the agentic IDE class is widely deployed without per-invocation auth gates.

CVE-2025-53767.name: Azure OpenAI SSRF Privilege Escalation (ZeroPath AI-discovered)

attack_vector.description: Authenticated low-privilege tenant user issues SSRF request that crosses the cloud-tenant boundary, escalating into administrative or cross-tenant context within the Azure OpenAI control plane. Microsoft fixed server-side before any public PoC.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low-privilege authenticated tenant identity
attack_vector.complexity: moderate
attack_vector.ai_factor: ZeroPath AI agent analysing Azure OpenAI control-plane attack surface. AI-defender against AI-service control plane.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection assumes east-west traffic within cloud-tenant boundary is filtered; SSRF crosses it.

EU-AI-Act-Art15.gap: Robustness control does not enumerate AI-service control-plane SSRF.

ISO-IEC-42001-AIMS.gap: AI Management System silent on managed-AI-service supply chain risk.

new_control_requirements.name: CLOUD-CONTROL-PLANE-CROSS-TENANT-CLAIM-VALIDATION
new_control_requirements.description: Cloud control planes must validate cross-tenant authority; managed-AI services inherit the same posture.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-53767 — SSRF crossed the Azure OpenAI tenant boundary into administrative context.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-DISCOVERY-CREDIT-IN-COMPLIANCE-EVIDENCE
new_control_requirements.description: Record AI-defender contribution to managed-AI-service security.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-53767 — ZeroPath against Azure OpenAI control plane; no compliance attribution path.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Pre-fix, every Azure OpenAI tenant globally was exposed; no operator-side compensating control existed. Post-fix exposure is zero — the compliance story is about telemetry coverage for future managed-AI control-plane bugs.

CVE-2025-10725.name: Red Hat OpenShift AI Privilege Escalation (ZeroPath AI-discovered)

attack_vector.description: Authenticated low-privilege tenant user leverages an RBAC primitive in the OpenShift AI platform overlay to escalate privileges across the control-plane boundary. The platform extends the conventional Kubernetes RBAC surface; tenant-isolation tests against the underlying API server miss the overlay.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low-privilege authenticated tenant identity in OpenShift AI
attack_vector.complexity: high
attack_vector.ai_factor: ZeroPath AI agent analysing OpenShift AI integration surface. AI defender finding bugs in AI-deployment platform — class anchor for AGENTS.md Hard Rule #7.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege enforced at conventional Kubernetes RBAC layer; AI-platform overlay extends attack surface.

FedRAMP-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement at API server assumed; OpenShift AI overlay introduces additional control plane.

EU-AI-Act-Art15.gap: AI-platform deployment surface not enumerated in robustness controls.

ISO-IEC-42001-AIMS-A.6.2.5.gap: AI lifecycle controls do not address managed-AI-platform tenant isolation.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-PLATFORM-CONTROL-PLANE-RBAC-OVERLAY-AUDIT
new_control_requirements.description: Tenant-isolation tests must exercise the AI-platform overlay control plane, not just the underlying Kubernetes RBAC.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-10725 — low-privilege user escalated via the platform overlay beyond what Kubernetes-RBAC tests would surface.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-DISCOVERY-CREDIT-IN-COMPLIANCE-EVIDENCE
new_control_requirements.description: Record AI-defender contribution against AI-deployment platforms.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2025-10725 — ZeroPath against Red Hat OpenShift AI.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: AI-platform overlay RBAC audits are essentially never performed; tenant-isolation evidence stops at Kubernetes RBAC depth.

MAL-2025-AI-FOUND-FFMPEG-BIGSLEEP.name: Big Sleep AI Open-Source 20-Vulnerability Disclosure Tranche (FFmpeg + ImageMagick + others)

attack_vector.description: Pre-emptive AI-agent discovery tranche covering FFmpeg, ImageMagick, and other open-source media / utility libraries. Bugs reported through standard upstream-vendor responsible-disclosure with patches landing before public PoC. Composite entry — per-CVE detail is being published by Google as the tranche disclosure unfolds. Representative path: memory-corruption in a media-decoder triggered by crafted file processed via library API.
attack_vector.privileges_required: varies per CVE in the tranche
attack_vector.complexity: varies per CVE
attack_vector.ai_factor: Big Sleep — Google DeepMind + Project Zero AI agent (Gemini-backed). First 20 vulnerabilities Big Sleep found, disclosed by Heather Adkins on 2025-08-04. Both targets (FFmpeg, ImageMagick) have massive downstream redistribution surface.

NIST-800-218-SSDF-PW.7.1.gap: Code review by automated tools — frameworks lack specific AI-discovery attribution model.

EU-AI-Act-Art15.gap: Framework does not enumerate AI-as-defender contribution to robustness control.

ISO-IEC-42001-AIMS.gap: AI Management System silent on AI-vulnerability-discovery as a positive control surface.

OWASP-SAMM-Code-Review.gap: AI-tooling not enumerated as a SAMM code-review modality.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-DISCOVERY-RESPONSE-SLA
new_control_requirements.description: AI-discovered vulnerabilities enter the patch program on the AI-discovery-date timestamp; downstream consumers of bundled FFmpeg / ImageMagick must monitor the Big Sleep disclosure cadence directly, not wait for NVD.
new_control_requirements.evidence: MAL-2025-AI-FOUND-FFMPEG-BIGSLEEP — tranche cadence outpaces NVD publication.

new_control_requirements.name: AI-DISCOVERY-CREDIT-IN-COMPLIANCE-EVIDENCE
new_control_requirements.description: Compliance evidence packs must record AI-tool contribution as a distinct positive control modality.
new_control_requirements.evidence: MAL-2025-AI-FOUND-FFMPEG-BIGSLEEP — Big Sleep tranche has no SSDF / SAMM / 42001 attribution path.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Bundled-FFmpeg / ImageMagick inventory across consuming projects is rare; AI-discovery cadence outpaces operator patch programs. Composite-entry rating — refine when per-CVE detail lands.

attack_vector.description: Missing copy-on-write guard in the Linux kernel rxgk_decrypt_skb function. Unprivileged local user writes to a read-only page via the page-cache CoW path, corrupting kernel memory and escalating to root. Same primitive class as Copy Fail / Dirty Frag / Fragnesia.
attack_vector.privileges_required: unprivileged local user; CONFIG_RXGK=y on the running kernel
attack_vector.complexity: deterministic, no race condition (PoC published 2026-05-17 by V12 security team)
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered. V12 security team rediscovered after the 2026-04-25 silent patch had already landed; the rediscovery is a parallel-discovery event, not an AI-assisted find. The Copy-Fail-class detection method (page-cache CoW guard absence) is what surfaces these patterns under human review now that operators know to look for them.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: 30-day SLA is exploitation window for class-of-bug PoC publications.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Appropriate timescales undefined; 30-day default is unsafe for kernel-LPE PoCs.

NIS2-Art21-availability.gap: Network-controls section silent on kernel-LPE; encryption-in-transit not a compensating control.

new_control_requirements.name: CISA-KEV-RESPONSE-SLA
new_control_requirements.description: 4-hour SLA on public-PoC kernel LPEs even without KEV listing.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2026-31635 — PoC published before KEV listing.

new_control_requirements.name: PRIMARY-SOURCE-INTAKE-VENDOR-BLOG-COVERAGE
new_control_requirements.description: Threat-intake pipelines must cover vendor security blogs (Microsoft / Sysdig / Trail of Bits / Project Zero) in addition to advisory feeds (Qualys / RHSA / USN / ZDI / kernel.org / oss-security / JFrog / CISA). Silent kernel patches with delayed-disclosure research publications fall through advisory-only feeds — vendor blogs are the canonical signal channel for that class.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2026-31635 — patched 2026-04-25 in mainline, V12 PoC published 2026-05-17 via BleepingComputer / news4hackers, fell through 8 primary-source feeds because the kernel.org Atom rolling window expired and oss-security never received the report.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: CONFIG_RXGK is only enabled on Fedora / Arch / openSUSE Tumbleweed kernels — enterprise distros (RHEL / Ubuntu LTS / SUSE Enterprise) on older kernel branches without rxgk are NOT affected. Exposure concentrated in dev/research workstations and bleeding-edge servers.

CVE-2020-17103-REREGRESSION-2026.name: MiniPlasma — Windows cldflt.sys SYSTEM EoP (re-regression of CVE-2020-17103)

attack_vector.description: Original 2020-discovered cldflt.sys TOCTOU LPE silently re-regressed in current shipping Windows binaries. Researcher (Nightmare-Eclipse) reran the 2020 Forshaw / Project Zero PoC against fully-patched Windows 11 and observed identical SYSTEM-shell outcome. Microsoft has not issued a new CVE — the 2020 ID is treated as authoritative — so standard NVD / KEV intake feeds will never surface this as a current threat without an additional detection method.
attack_vector.privileges_required: unprivileged local user; cldflt.sys loaded (default on all Windows 10/11 with cloud-sync support)
attack_vector.complexity: low — single-binary PoC, no race tuning
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered. Detection method that would have caught it: NEW-CTRL-074 CVE-regression-watcher — scans PoC announcements for historical CVE-YYYY-NNN references that imply silent re-breakage.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: 30-day SLA assumes a patch exists. The re-regression class has no patch at PoC-publish time — SI-2 has no provision for 'fix-shipped-but-reverted' state.

NIST-800-53-CM-3.gap: Configuration-change control assumes regressions come from operator drift, not from vendor silently undoing a previously-shipped fix. CM-3 has no surfacing mechanism for the regression class.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Vulnerability management standard does not name 'vendor regression of a previously-fixed CVE' as a distinct class.

new_control_requirements.name: RESEARCHER-HANDLE-ACTIVITY-TRACKER
new_control_requirements.description: When a researcher handle is named in any catalog entry's discovery_attribution_note or poc_description, their public code-platform activity (GitHub events or a GitLab activity feed) must be polled by the threat-intake pipeline. The handle becomes a known signal source after a single catalog-grade drop and warrants prioritized surfacing of subsequent drops.
new_control_requirements.evidence: CVE-2020-17103-REREGRESSION-2026, BUG-2026-NIGHTMARE-ECLIPSE-YELLOWKEY, BUG-2026-NIGHTMARE-ECLIPSE-GREENPLASMA, BUG-2026-NIGHTMARE-ECLIPSE-UNDEFEND all share the Nightmare-Eclipse / Chaotic Eclipse handle whose prior drop (BlueHammer / CVE-2026-33825) is already in the catalog. The 12-feed intake polled the right RSS sources but had no handle-tracker, so four catalog-grade drops accumulated unobserved over 5 weeks.

new_control_requirements.name: CVE-REGRESSION-WATCHER
new_control_requirements.description: Threat-intake pipelines must surface PoC announcements that reference historical CVE IDs (CVE-YYYY-NNN where YYYY <= current_year - 2) as candidate silent-regression cases. The historical-CVE-in-PoC-text class would have caught MiniPlasma's re-regression at T+0 — the original CVE-2020-17103 is cited in every news writeup but no advisory feed surfaces 'CVE-2020 references appearing in 2026 PoCs'.
new_control_requirements.evidence: MiniPlasma is the canonical anchor — published 2026-05-13 with CVE-2020-17103 cited in every news write-up and the original Forshaw blog post linked from the researcher's GitHub release. A regression-watcher pattern (extract historical-CVE references from poller diffs → cross-check against catalog) would have surfaced this at T+0.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: cldflt.sys is loaded on the global Windows 10/11 fleet with cloud-sync support — effectively every enterprise endpoint. No vendor patch as of catalog entry; operator-side mitigation (unloading the filter) breaks OneDrive / SharePoint sync, so most organizations remain exposed pending the next Patch Tuesday cumulative. Compliance audit posture: passing on SI-2 (no patch missing yet because no patch shipped), failing in operational reality.

BUG-2026-NIGHTMARE-ECLIPSE-YELLOWKEY.name: YellowKey — Windows BitLocker TPM-Only Bypass via WinRE + USB Media (Nightmare-Eclipse)

attack_vector.description: YellowKey chains Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE) entry with USB-tooling load to inherit the TPM-unsealed BitLocker VMK on TPM-only-protector configurations. The TPM completes unsealing during pre-OS boot; WinRE inherits the unlocked-volume cache; an attacker-supplied USB stick supplies tooling that WinRE loads in a context with VMK access. Full read + write on the BitLocker-protected partition without recovery-key prompt or PIN entry. Physical-access vector (AV:P); operationally suitable for evil-maid / stolen-laptop scenarios.
attack_vector.privileges_required: physical access to a powered-off / sleep BitLocker-protected device
attack_vector.complexity: low — single-binary PoC
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered. The control gap is structural — BitLocker TPM-only is documented to trade convenience for evil-maid resistance; YellowKey demonstrates the practical cost of that trade is higher than the GPO default acknowledges.

NIST-800-53-MP-7.gap: Media protection assumes encryption-at-rest defeats a thief. YellowKey breaks this for the default Windows BitLocker configuration.

NIST-800-53-SC-28.gap: Protection of information at rest assumes the key is unrecoverable without authentication; TPM-only protector silently violates this.

PCI-DSS-4.0-3.5.gap: Cryptographic-key protection presumes keys are not accessible without authentication; TPM-only-protected BitLocker fails for the YellowKey bypass class.

new_control_requirements.name: RESEARCHER-HANDLE-ACTIVITY-TRACKER
new_control_requirements.description: Same handle-tracker control as MiniPlasma — Nightmare-Eclipse drop.
new_control_requirements.evidence: Same researcher; same intake-pipeline gap.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Domain-joined devices with TPM+PIN protector enforced by Group Policy are NOT affected. Consumer + unmanaged-enterprise devices (BYOD, contractor laptops, satellite-office workstations) are the exposed population.

BUG-2026-NIGHTMARE-ECLIPSE-GREENPLASMA.name: GreenPlasma — Windows CTFMON Trust-Abuse Local Privilege Escalation (Nightmare-Eclipse)

attack_vector.description: GreenPlasma chains arbitrary-memory-section-object creation in SYSTEM-writable directory paths (via registry + object-manager-permission primitives) with CTFMON's implicit-trust pathway to escalate from unprivileged user to SYSTEM. The trust assumption is architectural — CTFMON runs in SYSTEM context and interacts with object-manager paths under the assumption that only SYSTEM can write them. The publicly-released PoC is intentionally incomplete (no SYSTEM-shell wrapper) but the primitive is fully documented and operator-completable.
attack_vector.privileges_required: unprivileged local user
attack_vector.complexity: moderate (released PoC is incomplete; primitive is operator-completable in hours)
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: 30-day SLA inapplicable for unpatched Windows LPE with public PoC.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege presumes a stable user / SYSTEM boundary; GreenPlasma demonstrates the boundary is breakable from a baseline account.

new_control_requirements.name: RESEARCHER-HANDLE-ACTIVITY-TRACKER
new_control_requirements.description: Same handle-tracker control — Nightmare-Eclipse drop.
new_control_requirements.evidence: Same researcher; same intake-pipeline gap.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Affected component not yet publicly disclosed; presumed broad Windows fleet exposure pending Microsoft advisory.

attack_vector.description: Local tampering primitive corrupts Microsoft Defender's update-pipeline state so that signatures + platform binaries silently age out of currency while the agent reports healthy. Combined with BlueHammer / RedSun (CVE-2026-33825), produces chained-defense-degradation + LPE state where the local AV cannot detect the SYSTEM-shell primitive.
attack_vector.privileges_required: unprivileged local user
attack_vector.complexity: low — single-binary PoC
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Assumes AV agent currency is verifiable from the agent's own status output; UnDefend breaks this assumption.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: Monitoring controls assume they remain effective; UnDefend silently degrades a primary monitoring control.

new_control_requirements.name: AV-AGENT-CURRENCY-CROSS-VERIFICATION
new_control_requirements.description: AV / EDR currency must be verified from a source independent of the agent's own status output. Get-MpComputerStatus is insufficient — UnDefend demonstrates an agent can report current while the update channel has been corrupted. Cross-check signature + platform timestamps against an independent control plane (Defender for Endpoint cloud telemetry, Intune compliance reports, SCCM software-inventory snapshots) and alert on drift > 7 days.
new_control_requirements.evidence: UnDefend (April 2026) — Huntress observed in-wild exploitation against Defender hosts where the local agent reported healthy.

new_control_requirements.name: RESEARCHER-HANDLE-ACTIVITY-TRACKER
new_control_requirements.description: Same handle-tracker control — Nightmare-Eclipse April-cluster drop.
new_control_requirements.evidence: Same researcher; same intake-pipeline gap.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Defender-enabled Windows population minus the slice running third-party AV. Huntress in-wild observations confirm operational exploitation.

CVE-2026-41940.name: WebPros cPanel & WHM and WP2 (WordPress Squared) Missing Authentication for Critical Function Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: missing authentication for a critical function (CWE-306) on the cPanel & WHM / WP2 management surface, reachable by an unauthenticated attacker. CISA KEV-listed 2026-04-30 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network reach to the affected service)
attack_vector.complexity: low — KEV-listed, actively exploited; treat as weaponized
attack_vector.ai_factor: No AI involvement documented in discovery or weaponization.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the cPanel/WHM update from the advisory; the hosting-control-panel class is internet-facing by function, so confirm the affected function is no longer unauthenticated and audit for unauthorized administrative actions.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is definitive once applied; the gap is the SLA — a 30-day cycle loses to the KEV-confirmed exploitation window.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Network/behavior monitoring on the cPanel/WHM management surface: requests matching the exploited weakness, and unexpected access, process execution, or new accounts on the service.
detection.adequacy: Necessary to catch exploitation of instances not yet patched within the compressed window.

response.what_would_have_worked: Patch immediately under the KEV SLA, then investigate for post-exploitation persistence and rotate credentials reachable from the cPanel/WHM management surface.
response.adequacy: Mandatory for a KEV-listed unauthenticated flaw; patch-in-place without compromise assessment can leave attacker persistence.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The 30-day flaw-remediation SLA is far longer than the observed exploitation window for a KEV-listed unauthenticated network flaw; CISA KEV due dates are days, not a month.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: 'Appropriate timescales' is undefined; the standard 30-day reading is unsafe for an unauthenticated, actively-exploited flaw on an internet-facing service.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Treats the service class as essential-function infrastructure but lacks a CISA-KEV-style compressed remediation SLA; operators learn of the flaw via vendor advisory, not a regulatory clock.

PCI-DSS-4.0-6.3.3.gap: The 30-day critical-patch window is exploitation acceptance for an unauthenticated flaw on an internet-facing system in or adjacent to the CDE.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Internet-facing WebPros cPanel & WHM / WP2 is routinely run by audited organizations on a standard 30-day patch SLA, which was active exposure for this KEV-listed, actively-exploited flaw.

attack_vector.description: a path-traversal flaw (CWE-22) letting an attacker write or read files outside the intended directory (used with the companion authentication bypass to drop a web shell). CISA KEV-listed 2026-04-28 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (the flaw is reachable by an unauthenticated attacker on the application's public interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — KEV-listed, actively exploited; treat as weaponized
attack_vector.ai_factor: No AI involvement documented in discovery or weaponization.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the ConnectWise ScreenConnect update; hunt for ASPX/web shells dropped via the traversal and rotate ScreenConnect credentials. ScreenConnect is RMM, so downstream managed endpoints are in the blast radius.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is necessary but, for these RCE/auth-bypass flaws, insufficient alone — web shells and stolen credentials survive the patch and require explicit cleanup.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitoring on the ScreenConnect: exploit-shaped requests, new web-shell files, unexpected process execution, and authentication/authorization events with no matching legitimate session or with forged key material.
detection.adequacy: Necessary to catch resident persistence and key abuse after patching.

response.what_would_have_worked: Patch immediately, hunt and remove web shells, rotate application secrets and credentials, and review data and downstream systems reachable from the ScreenConnect; assume compromise of accounts and managed endpoints in its reach.
response.adequacy: Mandatory; patch-in-place without key rotation / web-shell hunting leaves the attacker resident or able to re-authenticate.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The 30-day flaw-remediation SLA is far longer than the observed exploitation window for a KEV-listed, unauthenticated server-side application RCE/auth-bypass; these are mass-exploited within days, and RMM/file-sharing compromise reaches downstream systems.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: 'Appropriate timescales' is undefined; the standard 30-day reading is unsafe for an actively-exploited, internet-facing enterprise application.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Treats internet-facing enterprise applications as essential-function infrastructure but lacks a CISA-KEV-style compressed remediation SLA, and does not require the web-shell-hunt / key-rotation cleanup these RCEs and key-disclosure flaws need.

PCI-DSS-4.0-6.3.3.gap: The 30-day critical-patch window is exploitation acceptance for an internet-facing enterprise application in or adjacent to the CDE; WAF coverage is partial mitigation, not remediation.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Internet-facing ConnectWise ScreenConnect is run by audited organizations on a standard patch SLA and is mass-exploited within days; the required web-shell hunt and key rotation are rarely part of the documented patch procedure, and RMM/file-sharing/MES reach amplifies the blast radius.

attack_vector.description: command injection (CWE-77) on the D-Link DIR-823X, giving an unauthenticated attacker command execution on the device. CISA KEV-listed 2026-04-24 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network reach to the device/service)
attack_vector.complexity: low — KEV-listed, actively exploited; treat as weaponized
attack_vector.ai_factor: No AI involvement documented in discovery or weaponization.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the D-Link firmware fix if available; if the device is end-of-life with no fix, replace it and isolate it from untrusted networks. Consumer routers in this class are common botnet-recruitment targets.
prevention.adequacy: Patch/replace is definitive; the gap is that the affected class is internet-exposed by function and (for consumer/EoL devices) often unpatchable, so network isolation is the load-bearing control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitoring for the router web interface: exploit-shaped requests, device crashes/reboots, malicious firmware, and outbound botnet/C2 traffic from the device.
detection.adequacy: Necessary to catch exploitation of devices not yet patched/replaced; edge devices are frequently recruited into botnets.

response.what_would_have_worked: Patch or replace the device, isolate it from untrusted networks, and rotate any credentials reachable from it; reflash firmware on indicators of a malicious-update compromise.
response.adequacy: Mandatory for a KEV-listed unauthenticated device flaw; firmware implants survive a reboot without a reflash.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The 30-day flaw-remediation SLA is far longer than the observed exploitation window for a KEV-listed unauthenticated network-device/service flaw; CISA KEV due dates are days.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: 'Appropriate timescales' is undefined; the standard 30-day reading is unsafe for an unauthenticated, actively-exploited flaw on an internet-facing device — and end-of-life consumer devices have no patch at all, so the only control is replacement/isolation.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Treats the class as essential-function infrastructure but lacks a compressed remediation SLA; consumer/edge devices in particular are routinely internet-exposed and unpatched.

PCI-DSS-4.0-6.3.3.gap: The 30-day critical-patch window is exploitation acceptance for an unauthenticated flaw on an internet-facing device in or adjacent to the CDE.

compliance_exposure_score.basis: Internet-facing D-Link DIR-823X router is routinely run on a standard patch SLA — or, for consumer/EoL devices, not patched at all — which was active exposure for this KEV-listed, actively-exploited flaw.

CVE-2024-7399.name: Samsung MagicINFO 9 Server Path Traversal Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: a path-traversal plus unrestricted-file-upload flaw (CWE-22/CWE-434), letting an unauthenticated attacker upload a file (e.g. a web shell) and traverse to it for code execution. CISA KEV-listed 2026-04-24 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (the flaw is reachable by an unauthenticated attacker on the service's public interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — KEV-listed, actively exploited; treat as weaponized
attack_vector.ai_factor: No AI involvement documented in discovery or weaponization.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Samsung MagicINFO 9 Server security update; hunt for web shells under the service's root and rotate service credentials — an upload/injection primitive leaves resident persistence the patch does not remove.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is necessary; the recurring failure is the SLA gap and the lack of web-shell-hunt / rebuild cleanup, plus long-tail unpatched estates (the MS08-067 lesson).

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitoring on the MagicINFO Server: exploit-shaped requests, new web-shell files, unexpected process execution, and service crashes consistent with memory corruption.
detection.adequacy: Necessary to catch exploitation and resident persistence on an internet-facing service.

response.what_would_have_worked: Patch immediately; for the upload/injection variants hunt and remove web shells and rotate credentials, for the memory-corruption variants rebuild the host; review for lateral movement.

CVE-2026-20122.name: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager Incorrect Use of Privileged APIs Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: an incorrect use of privileged APIs (CWE-648) reachable by an unauthenticated attacker, enabling privileged actions on the management plane. CISA KEV-listed 2026-04-20 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network reach to the affected system)

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager fixed release; restrict the management plane to trusted networks and review for unauthorized privileged actions.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is definitive once applied; the gap is the SLA — a 30-day cycle loses to the KEV-confirmed exploitation window.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitoring on the SD-WAN Manager management plane: requests matching the exploited weakness and unexpected privileged actions, data access, or new accounts on the system.
detection.adequacy: Necessary to catch exploitation of instances not yet patched within the compressed window.

response.what_would_have_worked: Patch immediately under the KEV SLA, then investigate for post-exploitation persistence and rotate credentials reachable from the SD-WAN Manager management plane.
response.adequacy: Mandatory for a KEV-listed unauthenticated flaw; patch-in-place without compromise assessment can leave attacker persistence.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The 30-day flaw-remediation SLA is far longer than the observed exploitation window for a KEV-listed unauthenticated flaw; CISA KEV due dates are days, not a month.
